## The canonical paradigms to learn rewards {.nav-intro}

<!-- **Inverse reinforcement learning** (IRL) learns a reward function consistent with provided demonstrations or other forms of feedback -->

<div style="display: flex; gap: 1em;">
<div style="flex: 1;">

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL)"}
IRL infers the underlying reward $R$ that explains observed expert behavior, typically by assuming the expert acts (approximately) optimally to $R$.

[@irl]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}.
:::

</div>
<div style="flex: 1;">

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Preference-Based Learning (PBL)"}
PBL infers a reward $R$ that is *consistent* with human preferences over pairs or sets of trajectories, actions, or outcomes, rather than from demonstrations or explicit feedback.

[@rlhf]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}.
:::

</div>
</div>

<!-- **Preference-based learning** (PBL) learns a reward function that is consistent with preferences collected from humans. -->
However, human feedback is **imperfect**.

- Inconsistency, suboptimality, and heterogeneity bias provided feedback.
- Humans are not computers: they have finite energy and are risk-averse.

Even pairwise comparisons, can vary over trials and individuals. [@exopbl]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}
<!-- <span style="display: block; margin-top: -0.8em;">

</span> -->
